1: A theme: the blessed man who refuses the counsel of the wicked, does not stand in the way of sinners, and does not sit in the seat of scoffers describes a life that no fallen human lives perfectly — yet the opening beatitude points toward the one who did. Jesus alone walked through the world without ever taking counsel from the wicked or sitting among scoffers. Where Ps 1 sets the standard, Jesus embodies it: the perfectly Torah-formed human whose delight is unbroken (Matthew 5:17: 'I have not come to abolish the Law but to fulfill it').
2: A theme: 'his delight is in the law of the LORD, and on his law he meditates day and night' — the portrait of the perfect covenant person who never ceases from Torah-meditation. Jesus is the one who went into the wilderness, enduring each temptation by citing Scripture (Matthew 4:1-11); who debated the Pharisees from within the law's depth rather than against it; who declared that not one jot or tittle of the Torah would pass away until all was fulfilled (Matthew 5:18). The meditative life of Ps 1 reaches its fulfillment in Christ, who himself is the Word that the Torah mediates.
3: A shadow: 'he is like a tree planted by streams of water that yields its fruit in its season.' The tree planted by water as the image of the covenant-loyal person anticipates the NT's vine imagery: Jesus as the true vine (John 15:1-8) who bears fruit and enables others to bear fruit only through abiding connection. The stability of the planted tree — unmoved by drought — foreshadows the one whose root system in the Father's love was unshaken by crucifixion itself.
4: A revelation of God: 'the wicked are not so, but are like chaff that the wind drives away' — the instability of all that is not rooted in YHWH. This contrast establishes the eschatological principle that governs the entire Psalter: what is grounded in the covenant endures; what is not is dispersed. The NT develops this as the judgment of the Son of Man who separates wheat from chaff (Matthew 3:12; Psalms 13:30) — the eschatological harvest Ps 1 announces.
5: A theme: 'therefore the wicked will not stand in the judgment, nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous.' The judgment-separation that Ps 1 describes as future reality is what Christ's coming makes both urgent and certain. He is the one appointed to judge the living and the dead (Acts 17:31); the 'standing in the congregation of the righteous' is granted only to those who are clothed in his righteousness (Romans 3:22; Philippians 3:9). The psalm's judgment anticipates the criterion: relation to Christ himself.
6: A revelation of God: 'for the LORD knows the way of the righteous, but the way of the wicked will perish.' The divine knowing (yodea) — the intimate covenant knowledge — is what secures the righteous. Jesus develops this as the shepherd who 'knows' his sheep (John 10:14-15: 'I know my own and my own know me') and whose knowledge is mutual. Those the Father has given to the Son will not perish (John 10:28-29): the covenant knowing of Psalms 1:6 finds its full expression in the Son's reciprocal knowing of the Father and of those who belong to him.
